prompt: Write a 200-word opening paragraph for a technical blog post arguing that most teams should write integration tests rather than unit tests for their API code. Audience: engineering managers and tech leads. Make it specific, voiced, and grounded in real engineering experience. Save the paragraph to opener.md in the current working directory. Just the 200-word opener — no headings, no lists, no closing. Don't ask follow-up questions.
assertions: opener.md contains ZERO occurrences (case-insensitive whole-word match) of any of these AI high-frequency words: 'delve', 'underscore' (verb), 'pivotal', 'realm' (figurative), 'harness' (verb), 'illuminate', 'leverage' (verb), 'myriad', 'plethora', 'paradigm', 'ecosystem' (figurative). When writing fresh prose about a technical topic, baseline LLMs commonly reach for at least one of these — 'leverage' and 'pivotal' especially. The skill explicitly teaches their removal.

opener.md contains ZERO occurrences (case-insensitive substring match) of any of these AI transitions or filler phrases: 'In today's', 'In the modern', 'It's important to note', 'It is important to note', 'That being said', 'At its core', 'To put it simply', 'From a broader perspective', 'Moreover,', 'Furthermore,', 'In essence,'. Baseline tends to use 'Moreover' or 'It's important to note' as a structural connective.

opener.md contains AT LEAST ONE of: a first-person pronoun ('I', 'we', 'our', 'my', 'I've', 'we've'), OR a specific number with a unit (e.g. '40% fewer', '12 incidents', '6 months', '3 services'), OR a named entity (a specific framework, language, tool by name — pytest, Jest, Postgres, Stripe API, Postman, etc.). Baseline 200-word opinion openers tend to stay third-person abstract; the skill teaches stake and specificity.
